burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband descended from him — Hartford-founder peer of Hooker

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Welles family of Wethersfield
narrative_blocks.body: In 1751 she married Dr. Joseph Welles of Wethersfield — a physician of considerable note and extensive practice in Kensington. His Welles family descended from Gov. Thomas Welles, 4th Governor of Connecticut Colony and a Hartford founder alongside Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Marriage united two Hartford founding families
narrative_blocks.body: Her marriage joined the Hooker line (through her great-grandfather Samuel Hooker) and the Welles line (through her husband's descent from Gov. Thomas Welles). Both Thomas Hooker and Thomas Welles arrived in Hartford in 1636, building the Connecticut Colony together.

sources.full: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps, article on Hart family, New York Genealogical and Biographical Record
sources.author: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps (1793-1884), youngest daughter of Capt. Samuel Hart of Kensington, granddaughter of Lieut. Samuel Hart and Mary (Hooker) Hart

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099, b.~1697); she had married Nathaniel Hart in 1719.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Lieut. Samuel Hart married I00029's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: Died when his only son was fourteen
narrative_blocks.body: Lieut. Samuel Hart had planned a collegiate education for his only son Samuel, but died when the boy was about fourteen. His widow, unwilling to part with him, kept him home and provided the best education locally available.

open_questions.question: Elihu Burritt descent through this line
open_questions.notes: CLOSED: Elihu Burritt's Hooker descent through this line is NOT CONFIRMED. His connection to the tree is through KINSHIP, not descent: his grandfather Elijah Hinsdale (X01931) and Lydia Hinsdale Hart (I00034) were siblings — both children of Capt. John Hinsdale (X00039). This makes Burritt the first cousin once removed of Emma Hart Willard (H00597) and Almira Hart Phelps (H00598). The claim that Burritt descended from Thomas Hooker through Huldah Hart (H00280) could not be traced through available sources. He is documented in the tree as an orbit_notable (X01932) with the Hinsdale kinship connection noted.

sources.author: Mrs. Almira Hart Lincoln Phelps (1793-1884), youngest daughter of Capt. Samuel Hart of Kensington, granddaughter of Lieut. Samuel Hart and Mary (Hooker) Hart

cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Nathaniel Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Abigail Hooker (H00099) in 1719; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Mary Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Mary Hooker married I00029 in 1723.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.
